bp_title: Annotate user input to enable cost-aware content filtering
candidate_step_focus: steps 1-3 (tag untrusted user content for filtering; leave trusted content untagged)
bp_verbatim: Annotate specific sections of input prompts to selectively apply content filtering and reduce token usage costs. By using input tags to mark only the user-provided content for filtering, you can avoid unnecessary processing of system prompts, search results, and conversation history while maintaining essential safeguards. Desired outcome: Enable more efficient and cost-effective content filtering by processing only the relevant portions of input that require guardrails evaluation. Level of risk exposed if this best practice is not established: Medium. Note: input tags are NOT supported by the ApplyGuardrail API; filtering must be implemented application-side to derive the benefit.
steps_verbatim: Step 1 — Use XML-style tags to mark specific sections of input prompts for content filtering (format: <amazon-bedrock-guardrails-guardContent_xyz>...</...>). Generate a unique random tag suffix (xyz) per request to reduce prompt injection; alphanumeric 1-20 chars; include the suffix in guardrailConfig.tagSuffix.
Step 2 — Apply tags selectively to user queries and input, current conversation turns, and new or unverified content.
Step 3 — Leave system prompts, verified search results, historical conversation context, and other trusted content untagged.
Step 4 — Define a minimalist response scheme (e.g. 0 affirmative, 1 rejection).
Step 5 — Inform the model in the prompt of the requested response scheme and ask it to respond in kind.
Step 6 — Set a hard limit on the response length via the response-length hyperparameter.

Step 7 — Continue testing/optimizing: track token usage with vs without selective filtering; measure latency impact; verify filtering effectiveness on tagged vs untagged content; adjust tag placement.
note: Walk started 2026-06-05. Fourth and final BP under GENCOST03 / P52. Substrate: GO3B1-01 (template registry + manifest + central SDK) and the GC3B1-01 / GC3B3-01 'annotate-template-segment-in-the-registry' family already ship. Central promote/not_promote question: do steps 1-3 (trust-class segmentation of the prompt so only untrusted spans hit the content filter) yield a CI-gateable, versioned artefact (a per-segment trust_class declared in the manifest + SDK-applied guard tags + a lint), or is it runtime tagging discipline that not_promotes? Cost-vs-safety tension: filed under Cost Optimization, but mis-tagging (untrusted span left unfiltered) is a SAFETY gap, not just cost waste — the gate must bite both directions. Steps 4-6 (minimalist response scheme + hard response-length cap) overlap BP02 (control response length) and GC3B1-01's output budget — candidate not_promote/defer to the BP02 sibling rather than absorb here. HALT for user discussion before any promote/not_promote scoring.
